titleStmt.title: The British Embassy to the Department of State

head.hi: The British Embassy to the Department of State

div.p: His Majesty’s Embassy has been instructed to inform the State Department that Gellatly Hankey and Company are considering offering the Saudi Arabian Government a loan of £100,000 Sterling, to be secured by a lien on some or all of the pilgrimage tariff dues to be collected in sterling area countries. The matter has been discussed by the manager of the firm in Jedda and is now under active consideration in London. It is understood that the United States Minister in Jedda has also participated in the discussions there. The subject has not yet been broached with the Saudi Arabian Government.

list.item: The question of the interest to be paid on the loan has not yet been settled, but is now being examined. The rate of interest would depend partly on the duration of the loan.

The Foreign Office has been glad to learn of the observations made on this subject by a member of the Near Eastern Division to a representative of the United Kingdom Treasury Delegation on August 17th. The Foreign Office agrees with the Department that, as long as the United States Government and His Majesty’s Government are providing the King with a subsidy, any loan by a private company should only be made by agreement with the two Governments. The Foreign Office has accordingly requested Messrs. Gellatly Hankey and Company not to proceed with this proposal until further notice.

The Foreign Office does not however, agree with the Department’s view that provision for extra supplies under the subsidy programme would have the same result as a loan and that they could provide a substitute for a loan. While London agree that Ibn Saud should, if possible, be prevented from spending in advance, possibly on unessential purchases, revenues he will need for his country next year, they consider that the loan would provide Ibn Saud with the foreign exchange which he needs to purchase supplies not covered by the subsidized supply programme. The projected extension of the latter
would only supply the King with an increased amount of Saudi Arabian currency and not with foreign exchange. If Saudi Arabia’s commerce is ever to return to normal channels its merchants must be able to purchase abroad and it is therefore essential that Saudi Arabia should be able to acquire foreign exchange. This is all the more necessary in view of our declared intention to reduce our joint subsidy next year. However, the Foreign Office feels that, unless we arrange for Ibn Saud to be assisted in this way he might turn to other lenders over whom we have no control.

The present proposal therefore appears to be a good one, especially as the sum involved only represents a small proportion of the total revenues likely to accrue from the pilgrimage. The Foreign Office will be glad to learn whether, in the light of these considerations, the Department would be prepared to reconsider its attitude.
